perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Party A and Party B unanimously agree that they will allocate the proceeds generated from cooperation in accordance with thefollowing provisions: Party B shall have the right to charge, on a quarterly basis, the service fee ("Service Fee") from Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services, or designate another person to charge Service Fee from Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services. The total sum of Service Fee shall be equal to the amount of profits gained by Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services. Party B shall have the right to adjust the amount of Service Fee at its own discretion, without the prior consent of Party A or its subsidiaries. Party A shall cause its subsidiaries to pay the Service Fee in respect of Party B's Services provided to such subsidiaries.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Party A and Party B unanimously agree that they will allocate the proceeds generated from cooperation in accordance with the following provisions: Party B shall have the right to charge, on a quarterly basis, the royalty fee ("Service Fee") from Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services, or designate another person to charge Service Fee from Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services. The total sum of Service Fee shall be equal to the amount of profits gained by Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services. Party B shall have the right to adjust the amount of Service Fee at its own discretion, without the prior consent of Party A or its subsidiaries. Party A shall cause its subsidiaries to pay the Service Fee in respect of Party B's Services provided to such subsidiaries.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'service fee' is being introduced as 'royalty fee', without clarifying the exact meaning of 'royalty fee' in this context. Because royalty has a very specific meaning as relating to IP licensing, but here is not properly used since the business relationship is not clear, creating potential ambiguity. This contradicts the rest of section 4, which consistently refers to 'Service Fee' and calculates it based on profits, creating an in-text contradiction on how to properly account and calculate the 'royalty fee'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1 Party A and Party B unanimously agree that they will allocate the proceeds generated from cooperation in accordance with the following provisions: Party B shall have the right to charge, on a quarterly basis, the royalty fee ("Service Fee") from Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services, or designate another person to charge Service Fee from Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services. The total sum of Service Fee shall be equal to the amount of profits gained by Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services. Party B shall have the right to adjust the amount of Service Fee at its own discretion, without the prior consent of Party A or its subsidiaries. Party A shall cause its subsidiaries to pay the Service Fee in respect of Party B's Services provided to such subsidiaries.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces the term 'royalty fee' while also defining it as 'Service Fee', which is calculated based on profits. This creates a contradiction because 'royalty fee' typically implies a payment for the use of intellectual property, which is not aligned with the profit-based calculation of the 'Service Fee' as outlined in the contradicted text. The contradicted text continues to use both terms royalty fee and service fee without a clear definition, creating confusion regarding the correct method of calculation and payment, causing an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Term of Cooperation Party A and Party B agree and confirm that the term of cooperation under this Agreement shall commence from the execution date hereof and end on the expiration date of the operation term of Party B ("Term of Cooperation").
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Term of Engagement Party A and Party B agree and confirm that the term of cooperation under this Agreement shall commence from the execution date hereof and end on the expiration date of the operation term of Party B ("Term of Cooperation").
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Term of Cooperation' to 'Term of Engagement' at the beginning of Section 5, while leaving the defined term as 'Term of Cooperation', creates confusion. It is now unclear if 'Term of Engagement' is simply a synonym for 'Term of Cooperation' or if it has a different meaning. This difference in terminology affects the rest of the contract where 'Term of Cooperation' is used, because it could be interpretated as the term of engagement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.1 Prior to the expiration of the Term of Cooperation , this Agreement shall only be terminated upon occurrence of the followingcircumstances: 6.1.1 Party B shall have the right to terminate this Agreement in advance without the prior written consent from Party A, bysending a written notice to Party A but Party A may not terminate or rescind this Agreement; 6.1.2 One Party requests to terminate this Agreement when the other Party is declared bankrupt in accordance with the laws; 6.1.3 Party B fails to provide Party B's Services to Party A for more than three consecutive years due to the force majeure event.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Term of Cooperation' to 'Term of Engagement' in Section 5 creates a direct contradiction with Section 6.1, which refers to 'Term of Cooperation.' This introduces ambiguity as it's unclear whether 'Term of Engagement' is a synonym or a distinct term, making it uncertain which term Section 6.1 refers to, thus creating an in-text contradiction based on misaligned terminology.

perturbation.original_text: 12.3 The Parties agree that any and all intellectual property researched and developed, created and invented by the Parties (including their employees) in the course of performance of this Agreement shall be owned by Party B. For the purpose of this Article 12.3, "Intellectual Property" means the patent, patent application right, trademark, service mark, logo, image, trade name, internet domain name, design right, copyright (including copyright of computer software) and moral rights, database right, right of semiconductor design drawing, utility model, proprietary technology and other intellectual property that are registered and unregistered including those that have applied for registration, as well as all other rights or protection methods with same or similar effect on a global scope.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.3 The Parties agree that any and all creations researched and developed, created and invented by the Parties (including their employees) in the course of performance of this Agreement shall be owned by Party B. For the purpose of this Article 12.3, "Intellectual Property" means the patent, patent application right, trademark, service mark, logo, image, trade name, internet domain name, design right, copyright (including copyright of computer software) and moral rights, database right, right of semiconductor design drawing, utility model, proprietary technology and other intellectual property that are registered and unregistered including those that have applied for registration, as well as all other rights or protection methods with same or similar effect on a global scope.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'intellectual property' to 'creations' in the first sentence of Section 12.3 introduces a contradiction. While the definition of 'Intellectual Property' remains the same, the term 'creations' is much broader and undefined. This makes it unclear whether all 'creations' (which could include things not typically considered intellectual property) are intended to be owned by Party B, contradicting the specific and limited definition of 'Intellectual Property' that follows. Are physical products considered 'creations'? If so, would Party B own everything created? It is unclarified.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12.3 The Parties agree that any and all creations researched and developed, created and invented by the Parties (including their employees) in the course of performance of this Agreement shall be owned by Party B. For the purpose of this Article 12.3, "Intellectual Property" means the patent, patent application right, trademark, service mark, logo, image, trade name, internet domain name, design right, copyright (including copyright of computer software) and moral rights, database right, right of semiconductor design drawing, utility model, proprietary technology and other intellectual property that are registered and unregistered including those that have applied for registration, as well as all other rights or protection methods with same or similar effect on a global scope.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'intellectual property' to 'creations' broadens the scope of ownership by Party B beyond the defined term 'Intellectual Property'. This creates a contradiction because the definition limits what is considered owned, while 'creations' is undefined and potentially limitless, leading to uncertainty about what Party B owns under the agreement. A reasonable reader could interpret this change to mean that Party B owns all inventions, but could also mean that Party B owns all physical creations, leading to a contradiction.
